%%KC_BLOCK%%<div class="kc-visual-plan" data-visual-plan-b64="" style="display:none;"></div>%%KC_END%%
# 1.7 Continuous-Time and Discrete-Time Systems

> **Section Objective:** Learn how continuous-time signals, discrete-time signals, and discrete-time systems are represented and connected through sampling.

## Concepts In This Section

- Continuous-time signal
- Discrete-time signal
- Uniform sampling
- Sample notation \(x[n] = x(nT)\)
- Discrete-time system
- C/D and D/C conversion

## 1. Continuous-time signals and systems

A continuous-time signal \(x(t)\) is defined for every real-valued time \(t\), not just at isolated points. The variable \(t\) ranges over all real numbers, and the signal has a value at every instant in that range. Typical notations include \(x(t)\) for input and \(y(t)\) for output.

A **continuous-time system** is one whose input and output are both continuous-time signals.

**Minimal example:** A microphone voltage waveform \(v(t)\) changes continuously as sound pressure varies — there is no gap or jump between one moment and the next.

### EXAM TIP

If the variable appears in **parentheses** — such as \(x(t)\) — the problem is almost certainly using continuous-time notation.

#### Common Misuse

Do not replace \(t\) with an integer index unless the signal has already been sampled.

$$\text{continuous-time signal: } x(t),\quad t\in\mathbb{R}$$

## 2. Uniform sampling instants

Discrete-time signals in this section arise from samples taken at time instants \(t_0, t_1, t_2, \ldots\) This formula states that consecutive sampling instants are equally spaced by the same **sampling period** \(T\).

- \(t_k\) — the \(k\)-th sampling time
- \(k\) — an integer counter
- \(T\) — the constant time spacing between successive samples

Use this when a problem says samples are uniformly spaced or taken every \(T\) seconds.

**Example:** If \(T = 0.01\) s, then consecutive samples are \(0.01\) s apart.

#### Common Misuse

\(T\) is **not** the sample value. It is the time spacing between samples.

$$t_{k+1} - t_k = T$$

## 3. Discrete-time systems and sampled-data processing

The discrete-time sequence \(x[n]\) is obtained by evaluating the continuous-time signal \(x(t)\) at \(t = nT\).

- \(n\) — integer sample index (\(\ldots, -1, 0, 1, 2, \ldots\))
- \(T\) — sampling period in seconds
- \(x(nT)\) — the actual continuous-time value at the \(n\)-th sampling instant
- \(x[n]\) — the simplified discrete-time notation for that value

**Worked example:** If \(T = 0.01\) and \(n = 5\), then \(x[5] = x(5 \times 0.01) = x(0.05)\).

### EXAM TRIGGER

When a problem asks for a sampled version of \(x(t)\), immediately write \(x[n] = x(nT)\) and substitute the given \(T\).

#### Common Misuse

\(x[n]\) is **not** a function of continuous time. It is a sequence indexed by integers. Do not write \(x[n]\) as if \(n\) can take non-integer values.

$$x[n] = x(nT)$$

%%KC_BLOCK%%<div class="kc-visual-meta" data-visual-kind="interactive_demo" data-teaching-role="concept_anchor" data-visual-use-b64="" style="display:none;"></div><div class="kc-interactive-demo" data-demo-b64=""></div>%%KC_END%%

%%KC_BLOCK%%<div class="kc-visual-meta" data-visual-kind="book_image" data-teaching-role="concept_anchor" data-visual-use-b64="" style="display:none;"></div>%%KC_END%%
![Figure 1.32](/figures/page-108-figure_1_32-1.png)
*Figure 1.32 shows how a continuous-time signal is converted to samples, processed by a discrete-time system, and converted back to continuous time.*
<div class="lesson-figure-description">The figure is a left-to-right block diagram. The continuous-time signal \(x(t)\) enters a block labeled C/D (continuous-to-discrete converter), which outputs the discrete-time sequence \(x[n]\). That sequence feeds into a block labeled 'discrete-time system', which produces the output sequence \(y[n]\). Finally, \(y[n]\) enters a block labeled D/C (discrete-to-continuous converter), which reconstructs the continuous-time output \(y(t)\). Small sketches above the signal lines illustrate smooth curves for \(x(t)\) and \(y(t)\) and stem plots for \(x[n]\) and \(y[n]\). Students should notice that the discrete-time system processes only the indexed sequence, not the original continuous curve directly.</div>

## 3. Discrete-time systems and sampled-data processing

A discrete-time system takes a **sequence of numbers** \(x[n]\) as input and produces another sequence \(y[n]\) as output. Both input and output are indexed by integers.

Connecting this to Figure 1.32:

1. **C/D** converts the continuous-time signal \(x(t)\) into the discrete-time sequence \(x[n]\).
2. The **discrete-time system** processes \(x[n]\) to produce \(y[n]\).
3. **D/C** converts \(y[n]\) back into the continuous-time output \(y(t)\).

**Example:** A digital filter in a computer may smooth noisy samples by computing each output sample \(y[n]\) as a weighted average of nearby input samples \(x[n], x[n-1], x[n-2]\).

### QUICK CHECK

If both input and output are written with **square brackets**, the system is discrete-time.

#### Common Misuse

Do not call the entire C/D → discrete-time system → D/C chain a purely discrete-time system. The middle block is discrete-time; the full chain also touches continuous-time signals at its endpoints.

$$x[n]\;\xrightarrow{\;\text{discrete-time system}\;}\;y[n]$$

---
**📌 Key Takeaways**
- Continuous-time signals use notation \(x(t),\ t\in\mathbb{R}\); parentheses signal continuous time.
- Uniform sampling spaces instants equally: \(t_{k+1}-t_k=T\), where \(T\) is the sampling period in seconds.
- The sampling formula \(x[n]=x(nT)\) maps integer index \(n\) to the continuous-time value at \(t=nT\).
- A discrete-time system maps \(x[n]\to y[n]\); C/D converts \(x(t)\) to \(x[n]\) and D/C converts \(y[n]\) back to \(y(t)\).

*Next, systems can also be classified as analog or digital depending on the type of signal values they use.*
